summary: Joe Reis argues that the 'modern data stack' era is over and we're entering the 'boring data stack' era, where the focus shifts from managing underlying technology to tackling long-neglected fundamentals like data governance, data management, and data modeling. He sees the rise of AI/LLMs as the catalyst forcing organizations to finally get their data house in order, since messy corporate datasets that barely worked for BI are completely inadequate for AI applications.
key_points: The modern data stack has solved most classical data infrastructure problems, making it time to focus on governance and modeling
AI and LLMs are forcing the data profession to finally address data quality issues that have been ignored for years
Data modeling needs to return to fundamentals - conceptual and logical modeling - not just physical models
The future is knowledge-centric rather than data-centric, blending structured and unstructured data
money_quotes: Boring is back. Because the focus isn't on managing the underlying technology anymore, we can now turn our attention to the boring stuff we conveniently ignored during the heyday of the modern data stack era - data governance, data management, data modeling, and other 'enterprisey' practices.
I saw first-hand how most corporate datasets were very messy, lacked sufficient quality, and barely worked for BI, let alone AI. The rise of LLM's poured gasoline on this hot mess.
It's like we forgot decades of formal data modeling practices because it's easier to just throw compute at the problem. Turns out, the problem doesn't simply disappear.
It's not just about data anymore, it's about turning data into knowledge that's believable and useful for humans and machines.
key_insight: The AI/LLM revolution is exposing years of technical debt in data quality and modeling, making the 'boring' fundamentals of data governance and conceptual modeling suddenly urgent and unavoidable.
